name: Huron River Bottomland, Shore Road North
description: The low, wooded ground between the Huron River crossing and the north edge of Sandusky — a mile and a half of elm and cottonwood forest threaded by a single dirt track and two informal paths that only the conductors know. The telegraph line runs along the main road on cedar poles, its wires audible in the wind.
atmosphericDetails: The telegraph poles here are new — the insulators still white and clean — and the wires hum in the northeast wind with a sound that Elias Cole has come to hate on a level that is almost physical.
The bottomland mud holds boot prints for hours; a group of seven people moving north would leave a readable trail to anyone with a lantern and the will to follow it.

A gandy dancer's tool cache — a wooden box of track spikes and a tamping bar — sits beside the ballast of the rail spur that runs parallel to the shore road here, left by a work crew that will return at dawn.
The river crossing is a plank bridge wide enough for a single wagon; the planks are wet from the evening's rain and the water below is running fast and dark.
